title: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
author: Rolley Haggard
orientation: Rolley Haggard argues that because the Incarnation occurred at Christ's conception, not at His birth, God Himself defined personhood as beginning at conception, making any law that merely restricts or regulates abortion an implicit denial of Christ's humanity in the womb. The piece frames abolition (not regulation) as the only response consistent with the meaning of Christmas.
audiences: Christian pro-lifer who supports incremental restrictions
Pastor preparing an Advent or Christmas sermon
Skeptic asking when personhood actually begins
Legislator weighing heartbeat or gestational-limit bills

questions.question: When does Haggard say the Incarnation actually occurred?
questions.audience: Pastor preparing an Advent or Christmas sermon
questions.answer: He insists the Incarnation took place at Christ's conception in Mary's womb, not at His birth in Bethlehem. The manger is the public unveiling, but the union of divinity and humanity happened nine months earlier at the zygote stage.
questions.quote: It is important to remember that the Incarnation occurred not at Christ’s birth, but at His conception in Mary’s womb.

questions.question: Why does the timing of the Incarnation matter for the abortion debate?
questions.audience: Christian pro-lifer who supports incremental restrictions
questions.answer: Because the moment God joined Himself to humanity is the moment that fixes when human life becomes sacred and legally protectable. If God treated a zygote as fully human in the person of Christ, then every zygote must be treated the same way in law.
questions.quote: Important, because it defines very clearly the point at which human beings become human beings (when they are actually made in God’s image), the point at which human life becomes sacred, and the point at which human life should be protected in law.

questions.question: How does Haggard describe Christ's transition from heaven to the womb?
questions.audience: Skeptic asking when personhood actually begins
questions.answer: He paints it as an instantaneous move from the throne of God to embryonic existence inside Mary, with no intermediate stage of sub-human or pre-human development. The God-Man existed as a single-celled embryo.
questions.quote: One moment He was at the right hand of God the Father as the Second Person of the Trinity; the next He was in the womb of the Virgin as the God-Man in embryonic form.

questions.question: What does Haggard say is the only acceptable legal response for a God-fearing person?
questions.audience: Legislator weighing heartbeat or gestational-limit bills
questions.answer: Total abolition of abortion, with no settling for restrictions or regulations. Anything less, he argues, fails to honor the dignity Christ conferred on humanity at conception.
questions.quote: If He thus dignified humanity at this earliest possible stage of human development, so must every God-fearing person – BY ABOLISHING ABORTION, not by merely *restricting* it or *regulating* it.

questions.question: Why does Haggard reject regulation-style pro-life laws?
questions.answer: Because regulating abortion legally permits the killing of some preborn children while protecting others, which functionally denies that all human life is sacred. The intent of the lawmaker is irrelevant; the effect of the law is what condemns it.
questions.quote: For to merely *restrict* or *regulate* abortion is to say (by mere force of inescapable effect, regardless of intent) that SOME preborn humans may legally be put to death.

questions.question: What provocative test case does Haggard use against heartbeat-style bills?
questions.audience: Christian pro-lifer who supports incremental restrictions
questions.answer: He asks whether a law would have permitted the abortion of Jesus Himself before Jesus had a detectable heartbeat. If such a law would have been monstrous when applied to Christ, the same law is monstrous when applied to anyone else.
questions.quote: It is to say that it would have been acceptable – lamentable, surely, but technically legal nonetheless – for Jesus to have been aborted under certain circumstances; for example, before He had a detectable heartbeat.

questions.question: Which Old Testament texts does Haggard tie to the Incarnation argument?
questions.audience: Pastor preparing an Advent or Christmas sermon
questions.answer: He invokes Genesis 9:6's prohibition against murder as the rule that takes effect at conception, and he labels regulation-style abortion laws as the "iniquitous decrees" condemned in Isaiah 10:1-2.
questions.quote: Laws that merely restrict or regulate abortion are “iniquitous decrees” (Isa 10:1-2).

questions.question: What does Haggard claim Christians have effectively done for the last fifty years?
questions.answer: By tolerating laws that regulate rather than abolish abortion since Roe v. Wade, the church has, in practical effect, repudiated the Incarnation itself. Acceptance of partial protection is treated as a denial of what Christmas means.
questions.quote: To fail to do this (as we have failed to do ever since Roe v Wade almost 50 years ago) is to effectively repudiate Christ’s incarnation.

questions.question: How does Haggard tie the Incarnation to the call to abolition?
questions.audience: Skeptic asking when personhood actually begins
questions.answer: He frames the Incarnation as a divine "clarion call"—God's own announcement that the killing of preborn humans must end. To honor Christmas is to push for immediate state-by-state abolition rather than continued regulation.
questions.quote: Christ’s incarnation is the death knell of abortion because it is the clarion call of abolition.

related.title: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
related.reason: Same author-adjacent argument that the Incarnation, paired with the imago dei, is the theological engine driving abolitionism.

related.title: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
related.reason: Concretely applies Haggard's heartbeat-bill critique to actual legislation that licenses pre-heartbeat killing.

related.title: Abolitionist Perspective on the Texas Heartbeat Bill: Three Quick Observations
related.reason: Another working example of why heartbeat-based regulation fails the conception-onward standard the article defends.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Shares the article's "death knell" framing and extends the rejection of regulation-style strategy into the post-Roe legal landscape.

related.title: Ohio Gubernatorial Candidate: Stop regulating abortion and abolish it!
related.reason: Translates the regulate-vs-abolish distinction Haggard makes into a political platform demand.

related.title: Luther, Athanasius, and the Need For Courageous Men to Abolish Abortion
related.reason: Draws on the same incarnational, historically Christian theology to call the church to abolition rather than half-measures.

related.title: Stay Steeped In Prayer As You Seek To Abolish Abortion
related.reason: Pairs the doctrinal urgency Haggard articulates with the spiritual posture activists need to sustain abolition work.
